statement.markdown:
You are given a list of tasks where each task has a start time, end time, and a profit value. Find the maximum profit that can be made by scheduling the tasks optimally.

You can schedule the tasks in any order, but you cannot schedule any task that overlaps with a previously scheduled task. You cannot schedule the same task multiple times.

Your task is to implement the dynamic programming solution and fix the existing bug.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input will contain the number of tasks (n). Then n lines will follow, each in the format 's e p' where s and e are the start and end times of the task and p is the profit of the task.
statement.outputFormat: The maximum profit that can be made.
statement.notes: The input will be such that it is always possible to find an optimal solution. You cannot reschedule the same task.

examples.explanation: Choose the first task and then the third task

hints.text: Think about the state that we should maintain in the memoization table.

editorial.title: Optimal Task Scheduling
editorial.intuition: The main idea is to sort the tasks by their end times and then schedule them in that order. We can use memoization to keep track of the maximum profit that can be made by scheduling the first i tasks.
editorial.bruteForce: Try all possible combinations of tasks and calculate the maximum profit for each combination.
editorial.betterSolution: Use memoization to keep track of the maximum profit that can be made by scheduling the first i tasks.
editorial.optimalSolution: Sort the tasks by their end times and then schedule them in that order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by induction on the number of tasks.
editorial.dryRun: Choose a task with the earliest end time, then choose another task that starts after the first task ends.
commonMistakes: Not considering the case where the input is empty
Not handling the edge case where two tasks have the same end time
